question_text: On admission of a new partner, the sacrificing ratio is used to determine:
explanation: The sacrificing ratio shows how much each old partner has given up from their share to accommodate the new partner. It is used to determine: (a) the proportion in which old partners receive goodwill compensation from the new partner, and (b) how the goodwill brought by the new partner is shared among the old partners. Sacrificing Ratio = Old Ratio − New Ratio (for each old partner).
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subject: accountancy

options.text: The share of profit the new partner will receive
How goodwill brought by the new partner is shared among old partners
The capital the new partner must bring

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner's share is decided by agreement. The sacrificing ratio determines how OLD partners share the goodwill compensation received from the new partner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the new partner's agreed share with the sacrificing ratio's purpose.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital brought by the new partner depends on agreement or proportional capital calculation, not the sacrificing ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up capital contribution with the purpose of sacrificing ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation profit/loss is distributed in the OLD ratio (among all old partners), not the sacrificing ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing old ratio (for revaluation) with sacrificing ratio (for goodwill).

question_text: A and B are partners sharing profits in 3:2. They admit C for 1/5th share. If C acquires his share equally from A and B, the new profit-sharing ratio is:
explanation:
C's share = 1/5. C acquires equally from A and B, so each gives 1/10.
A's new share = 3/5 − 1/10 = 6/10 − 1/10 = 5/10 = 1/2.
B's new share = 2/5 − 1/10 = 4/10 − 1/10 = 3/10.
C's share = 1/5 = 2/10.
New ratio = 5:3:2.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: New ratio when sacrifice is equal
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: 3:2:1 would mean C gets 1/6 share. But C's share is 1/5 = 2/10. In the ratio 3:2:1, C would get only 1/6 ≠ 1/5.
elimination_hints.misconception: Simply appending a smaller number without computing the actual sacrifice.

elimination_hints.hint: 6:4:2 simplifies to 3:2:1, giving C a 1/6 share instead of the agreed 1/5.
elimination_hints.misconception: Same error as B, just written differently.

elimination_hints.hint: 5:4:1 gives C = 1/10, which is less than the agreed 1/5. A's sacrifice should equal B's sacrifice, each giving 1/10.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly computing the sacrifice amounts from A and B.

question_text: When a new partner is admitted, which of the following is NOT a right acquired by the new partner?
explanation: On admission, a new partner acquires: (1) share in future profits, (2) share in the assets of the firm, and (3) right to participate in management. However, a new partner does NOT automatically get a right to the past profits of the firm. Past profits (accumulated reserves, P&L balance) belong to old partners and are distributed among them in the old ratio before admission.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subject: accountancy

options.text: Share in future profits of the firm
Share in the assets of the firm
Right to past undistributed profits of the firm

elimination_hints.hint: Share in future profits is the primary right acquired by the new partner. This is what the new PSR governs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking profit sharing is not a right of the new partner.

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner does acquire a share in firm's assets proportionate to their capital and share. This is a standard right.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing asset sharing as a right of partnership.

elimination_hints.hint: Every partner has the right to participate in management of the firm. The new partner acquires this right upon admission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking new partners are excluded from management.

question_text: P and Q share profits in 7:3. R is admitted for 3/7th share which he acquires 2/7th from P and 1/7th from Q. The new ratio is:
explanation:
P's old share = 7/10. P gives 2/7 to R. P's new share = 7/10 − 2/7 = 49/70 − 20/70 = 29/70.
Q's old share = 3/10. Q gives 1/7 to R. Q's new share = 3/10 − 1/7 = 21/70 − 10/70 = 11/70.
R's share = 3/7 = 30/70.
New ratio = 29:11:30.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: 7:3:3 assumes old ratio carries forward and C simply gets 3 parts. But P and Q sacrifice differently (2/7 and 1/7), so their new shares change by different amounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming old ratio stays intact and new partner's share is simply added.
Rough estimation instead of precise fraction calculation.

elimination_hints.hint: 20:10:30 gives P = 20/60 = 1/3 and Q = 10/60 = 1/6. Check: P's correct share = 29/70, Q = 11/70. These don't match 20:10.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in subtracting fractions with different denominators.

question_text: Goodwill of the firm is valued at ₹2,00,000. C is admitted for 1/4th share and brings ₹50,000 as his share of goodwill (premium). This premium is:
explanation: C's share of goodwill = 1/4 of ₹2,00,000 = ₹50,000. C has brought exactly his proportionate share as premium. This ₹50,000 is credited to the old partners' capital accounts in the SACRIFICING ratio (not old ratio). The sacrificing ratio determines who gave up share for C, so they receive the compensation. Entry: Dr. Cash/Bank ₹50,000, Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (sacrificing ratio) ₹50,000.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Credited to all partners in old ratio
Credited to old partners in sacrificing ratio
Credited to the new partner's capital account

elimination_hints.hint: Old ratio is used for revaluation and reserve distribution. Goodwill premium goes to old partners in SACRIFICING ratio — because they gave up share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing old ratio (for reserves/revaluation) with sacrificing ratio (for goodwill).

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner PAYS the premium — they don't receive it. The premium compensates the old partners who sacrificed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Crediting the payer instead of the receivers of goodwill premium.

elimination_hints.hint: Cash received as premium is credited to old partners' capitals, not to Goodwill Account. Goodwill account is raised only if goodwill is to appear in books.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating a Goodwill Account when only cash premium is received.

question_text: A and B share profits in 3:2. C is admitted for 1/4th share. C brings ₹1,00,000 as capital and ₹40,000 as goodwill premium in cash. If the sacrifice is from A and B in their old ratio, the goodwill premium is distributed as:
explanation:
C's premium = ₹40,000. Sacrifice is from A and B in old ratio 3:2.
A's share of premium = 40,000 × 3/5 = ₹24,000.
B's share of premium = 40,000 × 2/5 = ₹16,000.
Entry: Dr. Cash/Bank ₹40,000, Cr. A's Capital ₹24,000, Cr. B's Capital ₹16,000.
Note: When sacrifice is in old ratio, the sacrificing ratio equals the old ratio.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Equal distribution (₹20,000 each) would apply only if A and B shared profits equally. They share 3:2, so distribution is in 3:2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Dividing premium equally instead of in the sacrificing ratio.
Using an incorrect ratio for distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: Giving the entire premium to A ignores B's sacrifice. Both partners sacrificed in 3:2 ratio and both receive compensation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Giving all premium to only one partner.

question_text: On admission of a new partner, if goodwill is to be raised in the books and retained, the journal entry is:
explanation: When goodwill is raised and retained in the books (appears in the new Balance Sheet): Entry: Dr. Goodwill A/c (with the full value), Cr. ALL Old Partners' Capital A/cs (in OLD ratio). This is because the goodwill was built by the old partners during their tenure, and all of them should benefit proportionately in their old profit-sharing ratio.
subtopic: Goodwill raised and retained in books
subject: accountancy

options.text: Dr. Goodwill A/c; Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (old ratio)
Dr. Goodwill A/c; Cr. All Partners' Capital A/cs (new ratio)
Dr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs; Cr. Goodwill A/c
Dr. Goodwill A/c; Cr. New Partner's Capital A/c

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio includes the new partner. But the new partner didn't help build the goodwill. Only OLD partners in OLD ratio should be credited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in goodwill credit — they didn't contribute to building it.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the entry — debiting partners and crediting Goodwill. Raising goodwill means DEBITING Goodwill (asset increases) and CREDITING partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing raising goodwill (creating asset) with writing off goodwill (removing asset).

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner did not create the goodwill. Crediting only the new partner makes no sense — the old partners built the goodwill and should be credited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing firm's existing goodwill to the incoming partner.

question_text: X and Y share profits in 3:2. Z is admitted for 1/5th share. Goodwill of the firm is ₹3,00,000. Z brings only ₹40,000 as premium instead of his full share. The shortfall in goodwill is adjusted by:
explanation:
Z's share of goodwill = 1/5 × 3,00,000 = ₹60,000. Z brings only ₹40,000. Shortfall = ₹20,000.
The shortfall is debited to Z's Capital Account (since Z didn't bring enough premium). The ₹40,000 cash and ₹20,000 from Z's capital are distributed to old partners in sacrificing ratio.
Entry for shortfall: Dr. Z's Capital A/c ₹20,000, Cr. X's Capital and Y's Capital (in sacrificing ratio) ₹20,000.
This ensures old partners receive their full goodwill compensation.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debiting old partners' capital accounts equally
Debiting Z's Capital Account for the shortfall

elimination_hints.hint: The shortfall cannot be ignored — old partners are entitled to full goodwill compensation for their sacrifice. The deficiency must be recovered from Z.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking goodwill shortfall can be waived without consequence.

elimination_hints.hint: Old partners are RECEIVING goodwill, not paying for the shortfall. The new partner Z bears the shortfall via debit to Z's capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Charging the shortfall to old partners instead of the new partner.

elimination_hints.hint: P&L Appropriation Account handles profit distribution, not goodwill shortfalls. The shortfall is a capital adjustment between partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Routing a capital adjustment through the wrong account.

question_text: A and B share profits in 2:1. C is admitted for 1/3rd share. C brings ₹2,00,000 as capital. The total capital of the new firm (including C) should be ₹6,00,000. The capitals of A and B before admission are ₹2,50,000 and ₹1,50,000 respectively. The capital adjustment required is:
explanation:
Total new capital should be ₹6,00,000. New ratio = A:B:C.
C's share = 1/3, remaining 2/3 shared by A and B. If sacrifice is from old ratio: A's new = 2/3 × 2/3 = 4/9. B's new = 2/3 × 1/3 = 2/9. C = 1/3 = 3/9.
Capital based on new ratio: A = 6,00,000 × 4/9 = ₹2,66,667. B = 6,00,000 × 2/9 = ₹1,33,333. C = ₹2,00,000.
A's current capital = ₹2,50,000. Required = ₹2,66,667. A must bring ₹16,667 more.
B's current capital = ₹1,50,000. Required = ₹1,33,333. B must withdraw ₹16,667.
Or: Adjust by A bringing in ₹16,667 and B withdrawing ₹16,667.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and B withdraw ₹25,000 each

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 adjustments are too large. A needs 2,66,667 − 2,50,000 = ₹16,667 more. B needs to return 1,50,000 − 1,33,333 = ₹16,667.
elimination_hints.misconception: Rough estimation without precise ratio-based calculation.

elimination_hints.hint: A needs MORE capital, not less. A's required capital (₹2,66,667) exceeds current capital (₹2,50,000). A must bring in, not withdraw.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming both partners need to reduce capital.

elimination_hints.hint: Proportional capitals are required. A has less than needed and B has more. Adjustment is necessary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the need for proportional capitals in the new firm.

question_text: When a new partner is admitted and the old partners sacrifice in a ratio DIFFERENT from their old profit-sharing ratio, goodwill premium is distributed in:
explanation: Goodwill premium is always distributed among old partners in the SACRIFICING ratio — regardless of whether it equals the old ratio or not. The sacrificing ratio shows who gave up how much, and they should be compensated proportionately. If sacrifice is in old ratio, then sacrificing ratio = old ratio. If sacrifice is different, the specific sacrificing ratio is used.
subtopic: Distribution when sacrifice differs from old ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Old ratio is used only when the sacrifice happens to be in the old ratio. When sacrifice is DIFFERENT, the specific sacrificing ratio must be used.
elimination_hints.misconception: Always defaulting to old ratio for goodwill distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio includes the new partner. Goodwill premium belongs to OLD partners who sacrificed — it's shared in their sacrifice proportions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in goodwill distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: Equal sharing is only valid if both old partners sacrificed equally, which is not always the case.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal sacrifice without calculation.

question_text: M and N are partners sharing in 3:1. O is admitted for 1/5th share which O gets entirely from M. Goodwill = ₹5,00,000. O brings ₹80,000 as premium. The treatment of goodwill will be:
explanation:
O's share of goodwill = 1/5 × 5,00,000 = ₹1,00,000. O brings ₹80,000 as premium. Shortfall = ₹20,000.
Sacrificing ratio: Only M sacrifices (entire 1/5 from M). N sacrifices nothing.
So entire goodwill premium goes to M only.
Entry 1: Dr. Cash ₹80,000, Cr. M's Capital ₹80,000.
Entry 2: Dr. O's Capital ₹20,000, Cr. M's Capital ₹20,000 (shortfall).
Total credit to M = ₹1,00,000. N gets nothing because N didn't sacrifice.
subtopic: Goodwill when only one partner sacrifices
subject: accountancy

options.text: ₹80,000 cash + ₹20,000 from O's Capital — all to M
₹80,000 to M and N in 3:1; ₹20,000 shortfall ignored
₹80,000 to M and N equally; ₹20,000 from O to both

elimination_hints.hint: N did NOT sacrifice any share (N's ratio stays at 1/5 before and after). Only M sacrificed, so only M receives the premium. Also, shortfall cannot be ignored.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing premium in old ratio when sacrifice is only by one partner.

elimination_hints.hint: Equal distribution to M and N ignores the fact that N made zero sacrifice. Goodwill goes to sacrificers only.
elimination_hints.misconception: Sharing premium equally without considering who actually sacrificed.

elimination_hints.hint: No Goodwill Account is raised here. Cash received goes directly to M's capital. Only ₹80,000 cash was received, not ₹1,00,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating a Goodwill Account instead of crediting the sacrificing partner directly.

question_text: When the incoming partner cannot bring goodwill in cash, the goodwill is raised through:
explanation: When the new partner cannot bring goodwill separately in cash, the goodwill is adjusted through capital accounts only (no cash transaction for goodwill). The goodwill is first raised in the books: Dr. Goodwill A/c, Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (old ratio). Then immediately written off: Dr. All Partners' Capital A/cs (new ratio, including new partner), Cr. Goodwill A/c. This achieves the same net effect — old partners gain, new partner's capital is debited for their share of goodwill.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Only debiting the new partner's capital account
Raising goodwill (old ratio) and immediately writing off (new ratio)
No adjustment is made for goodwill

elimination_hints.hint: Only debiting the new partner is incomplete — old partners must be credited too. The raise-and-write-off method handles both sides.
elimination_hints.misconception: Making a one-sided entry without crediting old partners.

elimination_hints.hint: P&L Appropriation Account handles profit distribution, not goodwill adjustments on admission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the wrong account for goodwill transactions.

elimination_hints.hint: Goodwill must be accounted for to fairly compensate old partners for their sacrifice. Ignoring it would be unfair to sacrificing partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking goodwill can be skipped if the new partner doesn't bring cash.

question_text:
Assertion (A): On admission of a new partner, goodwill brought by the new partner in cash is credited to old partners in the sacrificing ratio.
Reason (R): The old partners who sacrificed their share deserve compensation for giving up their future profit share.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — goodwill premium brought in cash by the new partner is credited to old partners in the sacrificing ratio. The Reason is also TRUE — sacrificing partners give up a portion of their future profit share, and goodwill compensates them for this loss. R correctly explains A — the sacrificing ratio is used because it identifies who gave up what, and they deserve proportionate compensation.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY sacrificing ratio is used — because partners who gave up share deserve compensation. Clear causal link.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the compensation logic with the ratio used for distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — the economic rationale for goodwill compensation is that sacrificing partners lose future profits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the economic basis of goodwill compensation.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — this is the standard accounting treatment for goodwill premium on admission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the basic goodwill treatment on admission.

question_text:
Assertion (A): When a new partner brings goodwill in cash and the goodwill is not to appear in the new Balance Sheet, only the premium entry is passed (no Goodwill Account is opened).
Reason (R): Opening and closing the Goodwill Account would be unnecessary when cash premium is directly distributed to old partners.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — when the new partner brings goodwill as cash premium and goodwill is NOT to appear in the Balance Sheet, we simply: Dr. Cash/Bank, Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (sacrificing ratio). No Goodwill Account is created. The Reason is also TRUE — since cash is directly received and distributed, creating and then closing a Goodwill Account is an unnecessary step. R correctly explains A.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY no Goodwill Account is needed — because the cash goes directly from new partner to old partners' capitals. The explanation is spot-on.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the efficiency argument as an explanation.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — creating and immediately closing a Goodwill Account when cash is directly distributed is indeed unnecessary bookwork.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a Goodwill Account must always be created when goodwill is involved.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — this is the most common treatment: cash premium directly to old partners' capitals in sacrificing ratio, no Goodwill A/c.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a Goodwill Account is mandatory in all goodwill situations.

question_text: On admission of a new partner, accumulated reserves and undistributed profits are distributed among all partners (including the new partner) in the new ratio.
explanation: FALSE. Accumulated reserves and undistributed profits are distributed among OLD partners only, in their OLD profit-sharing ratio. These reserves were built up before the new partner joined, so the new partner has no claim on them. The new partner shares only in future profits, not past accumulations.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Reserves belong to OLD partners — they were accumulated before the new partner's admission. Distribution is in OLD ratio among old partners only.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in distribution of pre-existing reserves.

question_text: If the new partner's share is 1/4 and the old partners A and B shared in 3:2, the new ratio (when sacrifice is in old ratio) is 9:6:5.
explanation:
FALSE. C's share = 1/4. Remaining = 3/4, shared by A and B in 3:2.
A = 3/4 × 3/5 = 9/20. B = 3/4 × 2/5 = 6/20. C = 1/4 = 5/20.
New ratio = 9:6:5. Wait — this IS correct! Let me re-verify: 9+6+5 = 20. A = 9/20, B = 6/20, C = 5/20. C's share = 5/20 = 1/4. ✓
Actually the statement IS true. The new ratio is indeed 9:6:5.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Verify: Remaining 3/4 split in 3:2 gives A = 9/20, B = 6/20. C = 5/20. Ratio = 9:6:5. Total = 20/20 = 1. C = 5/20 = 1/4. All correct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not verifying the arithmetic and assuming the ratio is wrong.

question_text:
Match the goodwill scenario on admission with the correct accounting treatment:

Column I:
(i) New partner brings full goodwill in cash; goodwill not to appear in books
(ii) New partner cannot bring goodwill in cash
(iii) Goodwill is to be raised and retained in books
(iv) Goodwill already exists in books at old value

Column II:
(P) Raise goodwill in old ratio and write off in new ratio
(Q) Dr. Cash, Cr. Old Partners' Capitals (sacrificing ratio)
(R) Write off old goodwill in old ratio first
(S) Dr. Goodwill A/c, Cr. Old Partners' Capitals (old ratio)
explanation: (i) Cash premium, no goodwill in books → (Q): Cash received, directly credited to old partners in sacrificing ratio. (ii) No cash for goodwill → (P): Raise in old ratio and write off in new ratio to adjust capitals. (iii) Goodwill raised and retained → (S): Dr. Goodwill A/c for full value, Cr. old partners in old ratio. (iv) Old goodwill exists → (R): Must write off existing goodwill in old ratio before any new adjustment.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This matches (ii) no cash → (S) raise and retain. But when partner can't bring cash, we raise and WRITE OFF (P), not retain (S).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing raise-and-retain with raise-and-write-off.

elimination_hints.hint: This matches (i) cash premium → (S) raise goodwill. But with cash premium and no goodwill in books, we use the simple cash entry (Q), not raise goodwill.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating a Goodwill Account when only a cash premium entry is needed.

elimination_hints.hint: This scrambles all four treatments. (i) cash premium = Q, not P. (ii) no cash = P, not Q.
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely mixing up the different goodwill treatment methods.

question_text: When a new partner is admitted and old partners sacrifice in their old profit-sharing ratio, the Sacrificing Ratio equals the _______ Ratio.
explanation: When old partners sacrifice for the new partner in their existing profit-sharing ratio, the Sacrificing Ratio is the same as the Old Profit-Sharing Ratio. This is the most common scenario. For example, if A and B share 3:2 and both sacrifice proportionately, the sacrifice is also 3:2. In this case, goodwill premium is distributed in 3:2 = old ratio = sacrificing ratio.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: When sacrificing ratio equals old ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio includes the new partner's share. Sacrificing ratio relates to what OLD partners gave up from their OLD shares.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing new ratio with sacrificing ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital ratio is based on capital contributions. Sacrificing ratio is based on the change in profit-sharing, not capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using capital ratio where profit-sharing ratio is needed.

elimination_hints.hint: Gaining ratio applies to partners whose share increased. Here we're talking about sacrifice (share decreased), which equals old ratio when sacrifice is proportionate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing gaining ratio with sacrificing ratio.

question_text: D and E share profits equally. F is admitted for 1/4th share. F brings ₹3,00,000 as capital and ₹60,000 as goodwill premium. Goodwill of the firm is valued at ₹3,20,000. F brings less than his full share of goodwill. How should the goodwill be treated?
explanation:
F's share of goodwill = 1/4 × 3,20,000 = ₹80,000. F brings ₹60,000 premium. Shortfall = ₹20,000.
Sacrifice is equal (D and E share equally and sacrifice in old ratio 1:1).
Entry 1: Dr. Cash/Bank ₹60,000, Cr. D's Capital ₹30,000, Cr. E's Capital ₹30,000.
Entry 2 (shortfall): Dr. F's Capital ₹20,000, Cr. D's Capital ₹10,000, Cr. E's Capital ₹10,000.
Total: D gets ₹40,000 (30+10), E gets ₹40,000 (30+10). Total = ₹80,000 = F's full goodwill share.
subject: accountancy

options.text: ₹80,000 raised as Goodwill A/c; F debited ₹80,000
₹60,000 cash to D & E equally + ₹20,000 shortfall debited to F and credited to D & E
₹60,000 to D & E; ₹20,000 debited to P&L Account

elimination_hints.hint: Shortfall cannot be ignored. D and E are entitled to F's full share of goodwill (₹80,000). The ₹20,000 shortfall must be recovered from F's capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting partial payment and waiving the rest.

elimination_hints.hint: Creating a Goodwill A/c for ₹80,000 and debiting F ₹80,000 doesn't reflect that F already paid ₹60,000 in cash. The treatment should show cash + capital adjustment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the cash premium already brought and creating a full goodwill entry.

elimination_hints.hint: The shortfall is between partners (F owes D and E), not a P&L item. It's adjusted through capital accounts, not the P&L Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a capital adjustment between partners as a business expense.

question_text:
Arrange the steps for accounting on admission of a new partner in the correct order:
(i) Determine new profit-sharing ratio and sacrificing ratio
(ii) Adjust goodwill (premium or raise-and-write-off)
(iii) Distribute accumulated reserves and profits in old ratio
(iv) Prepare Revaluation Account and transfer profit/loss in old ratio
(v) Record capital brought in by new partner
explanation: Correct order: (1) Determine new ratio and sacrificing ratio (i) — foundational step. (2) Distribute accumulated reserves in old ratio (iii) — settle old claims. (3) Prepare Revaluation Account in old ratio (iv) — adjust asset values. (4) Adjust goodwill (ii) — compensate sacrificing partners. (5) Record new partner's capital (v) — bring in the new partner. Order: (i) → (iii) → (iv) → (ii) → (v).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Recording capital (v) should be the LAST step, not the first. You need to determine ratios and make all adjustments before bringing in new capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Starting with the new partner's capital before any adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: Goodwill adjustment (ii) before reserves (iii) and revaluation (iv) is incorrect. Reserves and revaluation must be settled first in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adjusting goodwill before settling old claims and revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: Distributing reserves (iii) before determining ratios (i) is impossible — you need to know the old ratio to distribute reserves.
elimination_hints.misconception: Making distributions before calculating the ratios needed for distribution.
